speaker: interviewer
text: F-E-D-C-K, This is roll four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kuba, you were telling me about living in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that short period of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when I came to Krakow, as I said, I tried to find somebody from my family. I didn't find anybody. We were supported by the Jewish communities in Krakow. They tried to make things as bearable for us as possible. And I met some friends who survived another camp. But also this is the time when I met my wife, Helen. And we start going out together. In August of 1945, one day there was a pogrom in Krakow. What happened, one of the building where we stayed, the few Polish hoodlum came to the building, try to break in, and try to kill some Jewish people. I saw, right after this incident, that there is no future for us in Poland. This, we cannot survive. We survived concentration camps, and we might be killed. There were other towns where actually survivors were killed by Polish people after the liberation. That time I decided that I want to leave Poland. I don't want to stay. And I convinced Helen that we should leave Poland and go to American zone, to Germany. And we did. Early 1946 we decided-- there was organization from Israel, people came in order to recruit young people to take them to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beriha. And they were trying to get young people out from Poland to Germany or Austria or Italy and take them to Palestine. Well, through this organization, we signed up. And we went to the Polish-Czech border. We were smuggled through the Polish-Czech border, then went to Czechoslovakia. From Czechoslovakia, Polish-German border, and we went to Germany. We arrived because we had some friends, which survived also Schindler's factory-- very close friends-- and they settled in the city of Bamberg, in Germany. And this was on American zone. I don't know if this is important, but I will mention that during the smuggling from the Czech border, I happened to be very helpful. Because, at one point, the border was closed, because the guide who was actually taking over people from Poland to Czechoslovakia was caught by the Polish border guard and put in jail. However, one of my friends who came from Russia as a Polish officer was assigned to this border. And I went to him, and I arranged for this man to be released actually from jail. So that's another achievement which I was very proud. And I got a lot of recognition from the Beriha for helping out to opening the border. But this was just a coincidence. When we came to Bamberg, we were assigned to a DP camp-- displaced person camp. And in this camp, I got myself a job. I worked for Joint Distribution Committee. Later on, I joined for UNRRA and IRO-- International Refugee Organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for the Joint?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the office. What I did-- the Joint Distribution Committee established a supply warehouse. Because we were getting rations from the IRO-- International Refugee Organization-- but this was really nothing to speak about. Those were all army surpluses. And it was very difficult to eat and to survive on this. And the Joint Distribution Committee was sending, from the United States, edible products, something what we really could enjoy. And we were Jewish people, getting rations from the Joint Distribution Committee. So I had a job working in this warehouse. And December 1, 1946, we decided to get married. And we married in Bamberg, Germany. Because I was working for the Joint, the Joint helped us out to have a really very nice wedding. And we had the very nice wedding and start--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did a rabbi marry you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Yeah, we had a really, really-- almost orthodox wedding. Because there were few rabbis in Germany, from even survivors, were very orthodox. And they made sure that everything is done according to the Jewish law. We live in Bamberg. We tried to go somewhere to leave Germany. And the whole world was closed. There was nobody wanted to accept us. We, first of all, registered to Israel, to Palestine. But they were also taking certain people. Once we got married, they didn't want to take couples. They rather wanted to take single people. Then we registered to Australia. We registered to Canada. We registered to United States, any place. Nobody. The whole world was closed. Nobody wanted to accept us. Finally, in-- one other thing. We married 1946. In 1948 my son was born in Bamberg, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we already had a baby. And with the baby, even they start sending couples to Palestine, they didn't want to accept anybody with small children. Because the war was still going on. So, finally in 1949, when President Truman signed the DP quota-- displaced persons quota-- that's when the door opened for us to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Learning the trade during the war-- and I have to give again Schindler the credit during the war, I learned a trade. I learned toolmaking, and I registered as a toolmaker right after the war, after I was liberated. And all my records were toolmaker. And toolmaker was a very good trade to come to the United States. So there was not much of a problem to assign us to any contract. Don't forget, at that time even, we had to have not only a visa-- the DP quota-- but we had to guarantee-- the Joint Distribution Committee-- we had to guarantee that we will not be any burden to the US government. We will not be on welfare or anything. So they had to have some job for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow, they made contact with some Jewish men in Lynn, Massachusetts, who issued the papers that he needs toolmakers. They assigned us to this contract. And that's how we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Lynn, Massachusetts, that was our first home. We came to Lynn. We were adopted by the Jewish Community Center in Lynn. And they really helped us out to establish. It was very difficult to get an apartment, so they set us up with the family. We had a room with the family, so we were already three people with the 10-month-old baby, we came. And they did everything possible for me to find a job. Because actually, this contract was just a fictitious contract. I was going with one of the members of the Jewish community. And finally I found a job. I started right in the basement, actually, of the factory working, making $0.75 an hour, having a wife with a child. However, in 1952, my second son was born in Lynn, Massachusetts. So we had the second boy. And it was difficult. But I was ambitious, I worked very hard. I performed well, and I was promoted that, after a year and a half, I became a foreman in the factory. So, really, things were turning out very well for me. I worked in this place for three years. And then, because the owners decided to move the factory out from Lynn, I got another job as a tool and die maker in Lynn. And it was very good, it was an excellent job. I worked three more years. However, my ambition was always to go back to school and go into engineering. And I learned from some people that there is a very good company by the name of IBM. And, just luck happened, they were looking for tool and die makers. I sent a resume to IBM. I was asked to come for an interview to Poughkeepsie. I was interviewed. And I was accepted right on the spot. Also, I started in IBM as a tool and die maker. But they recognized my ability and talent. They sent me to school. And I have an Associate Degree of Engineering, and I got promoted a few times in IBM. I worked 32 years for IBM, as an advisory engineer and retired six years ago. My family consists of my wife with two sons. They're both married. They're both professionals. They are both lawyers. They're both practicing law. We have four grandchildren. We have-- one son has two girls. And one son has a girl and a boy. So that's our story, basically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kuba--

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I just want to mention that, for many years, we tried to tell the story of what happened to us. People didn't want to listen to us. People didn't want to-- not because, I don't think they didn't want to listen because they didn't want to hear about it. They felt that maybe they're going to bring up the bad things, the painful things. And maybe that why they said, don't talk about it. But number, number of years ago, when the first deniers came out and start telling that this never happened, we had a rabbi in Poughkeepsie by the name of Erwin Simon, very nice man, very powerful, and he encouraged us to go out and talk about it. And this was about 20-some years ago, when we start talking. And we're lecturing on a local level. And now, basically, when the movie came up and so much interest is about Schindlers and about the Holocaust, we are lecturing on, again, wherever we are invited to-- churches, high schools, colleges, organizations and so. Because we feel that this is our mission. We survived, in order to tell the world what happened to the six million, to the million and a half children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kuba, I would like if you'd mention-- you do a great deal for Federation. Would you discuss that briefly, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since I found out-- and I learned on my own from my own experience-- that it's much better to be on the giving side than on the taking side. So, as soon as I was able to give-- only not money, because money, I didn't have much. I had two children. I had to think of education-- but I did my best. But, in addition to money, I give of myself. Because they needed always volunteers. They need people to do work. And I always felt, for many, many years-- even up to today-- I volunteer in Poughkeepsie. And I was asked here-- we were asked actually, my wife and myself, we were asked to help out in the campaign here in Florida and did many, many speaking engagements. And I think it helped to raise money. Because this job is never finished. We were taken care of. There are many, many people that have to be taken care of today. And they need your help. And we need to help. Everybody should do his part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Kuba. Your story is incredible. And you told it so well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Well now, at this point, my wife, Helen, joins me in this interview. I was lucky. When I came back to Poland, I had to start my life back. I was very eager to start some life and find somebody, with whom I can share my life. I was very lucky to find Helen. And, as I mentioned, we were married in 1946. And next year, we're going to celebrate our 50th anniversary. And I hope Helen can add a few words.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is very strange even to mention, because my husband was recalling those years-- the difficult, dark years. At certain times, we went in some camps together. We never knew each other. We never spoke one word. How it was meant to survive for us, to be married, to raise a family, to be the continuation from generation to generation, to our Jewish tradition that our children, hopefully, will continue with dignity and honor befitting our people. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture you can see my mother, Sabina, my father, Abraham, my older brother, Emanuel, and myself, Kuba, when I was 1 year old. This photo was taken in 1923. My brother, Emanuel, myself, Kuba, and my youngest brother, Isidor, who was born in 1929. My last name is Beck. The photo was taken in Poland, in Krakow, in 1930. My mother, Sabina, my father, Adolf, my-- Abraham, the same. My brother, Emanuel, and myself, Kuba. We were at summer vacation in Poland. This was about 1935. My mother, Sabina, my brother, Emanuel, my brother, Isidor, and myself, Kuba, back. Picture taken in the wintertime, on winter vacation in Zakopane, in Poland in 1935. My brother, Emanuel, and myself, Kuba Beck. It was-- taking picture in Poland in 1935, with a bicycle. This is the last picture of my family, taken in 1938. In this picture, there's my mother, Sabina, my father, Abraham, my brother, Emanuel, my youngest brother, Isidor, and myself, Kuba Beck. This picture was taken in Poland in 1938. In 1979, when we went visiting Israel, we made our business to go to the Christian cemetery in Jerusalem, to visit Oskar Schindler's grave. For us, this was a very important event, to be there and honor-- by our presence-- to honor Oskar Schindler at his gravesite. In this picture, you can see my son, two daughters-in-law, and our four grandchildren and also us. This picture was taken in 1993 in Milwaukee, during bat mitzvah ceremony. In this picture, our four grandchildren-- Sarah Beck, Alison Beck, Stacey Beck, and Jeremy Beck. Sarah and Alison are two sisters. Stacey and Jeremy are sister and brother. And Sarah and Alison live upstate New York, and Stacey and Jeremy in Milwaukee. The place this picture was taken in Milwaukee, in 1995, during Stacey's bat mitzvah--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not '95.

speaker: interviewee
text: '3. Those are two gifts, which I received from the Federation Office in Poughkeepsie. I am very active for a number, a number of years. I'm very active in the Federation because, for me, it's a very important job to perform. They need always a lot of volunteers. And I feel that it's much better to be on the giving side than the taking side. I'm one of the volunteers who puts a lot of time and effort, in order to raise money for needy people.
